content.text:
[Sun 2026-04-12 16:13 GMT+2] [Subagent Context] You are running as a subagent (depth 1/1). Results auto-announce to your requester; do not busy-poll for status.

[Subagent Task]: You are a content strategist working with an editor (Dawn) on an article titled "How to Fire a Contractor Without Losing What Matters."

The article covers:
- Why most founders already know a contractor should be fired but hesitate
- The hidden math: bad endings cost more in lost work, legal exposure, and team morale than the original hire
- The threshold question: when firing is genuinely worth it vs. when replacement math doesn't favor it
- The termination process: contract review, documentation, four written confirmations
- Legal and operational protections: IP, non-disparagement, confidentiality, mutual release
- Why micro-business owners can't reliably implement this themselves
- Managed exit concept and the structural partner solution (AbroadWorks)

Your task: Write 2-3 different HOOK/INTRO options for this article. Each option is exactly 2 paragraphs. The hook should:
- Stop the scroll (open with tension, not a温柔 lead)
- Be specific and concrete, not abstract
- Imply the stakes without summarizing the article
- NOT use "this article" or "in this article"
- NOT mention the brand (AbroadWorks) — brand comes later in the article
- Be edgy and bold where appropriate — challenge assumptions

Format each option as:
**Option 1:** [title]
Paragraph 1...
Paragraph 2...

Do not include H2.1 or any structural headers. Just the raw hook/intro.

Output all options, then stop.

**Option 1: The Know-Know**
You already know. You've known for three weeks, maybe three months. The missed deadlines stopped being surprises a long time ago — they're just the background hum of your operating life now. You've rewritten the scope twice, had the "serious conversation" once, and sent two messages that walked right up to the line without crossing it. You know this contractor should go. You just haven't done it yet.

That's not a personality problem. That's a math problem you haven't run. Most founders treat firing like a confrontation — something that requires courage or closure or the right moment. It doesn't. It requires arithmetic. The moment you do the actual math on what a bad contractor costs you in drafts that go nowhere, legal exposure you can't name, and the attention bandwidth you keep leaking — the hesitation evaporates. The only question left is whether you know how to end it without creating three new problems in the process.

---

**Option 2: The Invoice You Can't Read**
Someone sent you an invoice last month that made you pause. Not because of the number — because of what it represented. Eighteen hours of work you couldn't account for. Deliverables that arrived sideways. A revision cycle that had no beginning and no end, just a contractor who seemed busy and a project that seemed to be happening and a feeling in your gut that something was off. You paid it anyway.

That feeling is data. Your time has a real dollar value. Your reputation has a real dollar value. The work that doesn't get done because you're babysitting someone who should be delivering has a real dollar value. Somewhere in your business right now, you're subsidizing a bad hire with your own labor, your own credibility, and your own sleep. Most founders do this math wrong — they count the direct cost of the contractor and ignore everything else. That's how you end up stuck, paying twice: once for the work, and once for the mess you can't quite bring yourself to clean up.

---

**Option 3: The Version of You Who Hired Them**
Remember the version of you who made this hire? Things were moving fast. The project was real, the deadline was real, and this person came recommended and available and said all the right things. You were optimistic. You were probably right to be. But that person — the one you hired — is not the person who's been delivering. And the work you thought you'd be getting by now is not the work that's sitting in your inbox.

The hard truth nobody tells you: most contractor problems aren't fixable mid-stream. The instinct to rescope, renegotiate, or coach your way to a better outcome is the same instinct that got you here — optimistic, forward-leaning, convinced you can will the situation into different results. You usually can't. What you can do is exit cleanly, protect what's yours, and avoid turning one bad hire into a long tail of legal ambiguity and unfinished business. That's not failure. That's math.
